data.title: Oriental Rise Holdings ineligible for grace period following reverse split; receives Nasdaq delisting notice
SKF delivers Q1 beat, says Iran tensions weigh on visibility
data.description:
Sweden’s industrial bearings maker SKF (SKFRY) reported a smaller-than-expected decline in first-quarter earnings, supported by resilient industrial demand and pricing, even as macro and geopolitical risks intensified.

SKF’s (SKFRY) Q1 net sales fell about 8.7% Y/Y to roughly SEK 21.9B, broadly in line with analyst expectations.

Adjusted operating profit was reported at SEK 2.95B, down from SEK 3.23B Y/Y, against a mean forecast of 2.74B in a poll of analysts provided by SKF (SKFRY). Adjusted EPS was SEK 4.25 (vs. 4.71 last year). 

"We expect market demand to remain at similar levels as in Q1 as a whole. Consequently, we expect organic sales to be relatively unchanged year-over-year, against more demanding comparables. However, geopolitical turmoil, including the conflict in the Middle East, amplifies overall uncertainty." CEO Rickard Gustafson said.

Outlook: The company guided for lower currency headwind in Q2 2026, expecting a negative impact of around SEK 100M on operating profit compared with about SEK 800M in Q1. 

The company maintained its FY 2026 outlook with a capital expenditure of roughly SEK 5B. It also reiterated that items affecting comparability tied to the Automotive separation and footprint optimization will total between SEK 2.5B and SEK 3B, unchanged and within the framework outlined at its CMD 2025.

SKF (SKFRY) pointed to geopolitical tensions, including the Iran‑related conflict, as key sources of uncertainty for order intake and capex decisions, especially in Europe and emerging markets, which in turn could weigh on future sales growth

data.title: Trump signals no extension of truce ahead of scheduled peace talks; oil down
data.description:
President Donald Trump signaled he is unlikely to extend a two-week ceasefire with Iran set to expire in two days, as Tehran has yet to confirm participation in talks aimed at ending the Middle East conflict and restoring global trade stability.

Trump told Bloomberg in an interview on Monday that the ceasefire expires on Wednesday evening in Washington, and he is “not going to be rushed into making a bad deal.”

He said the Strait of Hormuz would stay blockaded for now, and “I’m not opening it until a deal is signed.”

Mohammed Bagher Qalibaf, Iran’s chief negotiator and parliament speaker, wrote in a post on X early Tuesday that “We do not accept negotiations under the shadow of threats,” and the Islamic Republic has been preparing “to reveal new cards on the battlefield.”

Oil prices were trading 1% lower on Tuesday, with WTI crude (CL1:COM) trading at $86.47/bbl at press time, and Brent (CO1:COM) was down at $94.73.

Dear Readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: AB SKF Non-GAAP EPS of SEK 4.25, revenue of SEK 21.87B; gives Q2 and reaffirms FY26 outlook
data.description:
• AB SKF (publ) press release (SKFRY): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of SEK 4.25.
  • Revenue of SEK 21.87B (-8.8% Y/Y). Organic growth of 2.4%.
  • Adjusted operating margin:13.5% (13.5%).
  • Net cash flow from operating activities: -SEK 446M (977). 

  • Outlook and guidance

    Outlook

    • Q2 2026: We expect market demand to remain at similar levels as in Q1 as a whole. Consequently, we expect organic sales to be relatively unchanged year-over-year, against more demanding comparables. However, geopolitical turmoil, including the conflict in the Middle East, amplifies overall uncertainty.

    Guidance Q2 2026

    • Currency impact on the operating profit: around MSEK –100, year-over-year, based on exchange rates as per 31 March 2026.

    Guidance FY 2026

    • Tax level excluding effects related to divested businesses and separation of the Automotive business: around 28%.
    • Additions to property, plant and equipment: around BSEK 5.
    • Items affecting comparability related to the Automotive separation and footprint optimization: BSEK –2.5 to –3. This is within the frame communicated at CMD 2025.

data.title: ABF to demerge Primark from FoodCo after review
data.description:
• Associated British Foods (ASBFY) to demerge Primark retail unit from FoodCo after strategic review. On completion of the demerger, ABF shareholders will hold shares in both listed entities.
  • The Board has reviewed the anticipated dissynergies, which in aggregate are currently expected to be below £45M. One-off separation and transaction costs are expected to be in the region of £75M.
  • The review of the group structure was undertaken in consultation with Wittington Investments Limited, ABF’s largest shareholder. Wittington is supportive of the proposed demerger and remains committed to maintaining majority ownership of both Primark and FoodCo.
  • The Board is confident in the long-term prospects of both businesses. Primark is a global business operating 486 retail stores across 19 markets, with approximately £9.5B of annual revenue and more than 83,0001 employees. 
  • It is intended that both Primark and FoodCo will be listed on the equity shares (commercial companies) category of the London Stock Exchange, and, given their scale, it is anticipated that both entities will be constituents of the FTSE 100.

data.title: Zions forecasts 7% to 8% net interest income growth under a no-rate-change path while advancing Basis agency lending deal
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Zions Bancorporation (ZION) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Chairman & CEO Harris Simmons said the company was “reasonably pleased with our performance and financial results for the first quarter,” citing “meaningful year-over-year improvement and continued progress against our long-term strategic priorities,” with Capital Markets positioned as “an important driver of fee income growth.”
  • Simmons highlighted an announced deal: “In late March, we announced an agreement with Basis Investment Group to acquire their Fannie and Freddie lending programs, related mortgage servicing rights and an experienced team supporting those platforms,” adding, “we expect this transaction will meaningfully enhance our ability to serve commercial real estate clients across the Western United States and beyond and to further strengthen our capital markets franchise.”
  • On consumer and small business deposits, Simmons pointed to product rollout: “Following the launch of our new gold account consumer deposit product in the second half of 2025, we recently introduced its companion offering for small business customers, branded as ‘beyond the business.’” He added the product “began piloting…in Colorado and Arizona late in the quarter” and “it’s expected to roll out more broadly across our affiliate banks later this quarter.”
  • Simmons cited SBA positioning: “We now rank 11th nationally in SBA 7(a) loan approvals during the first half of the SBA’s fiscal year.”
  • Executive VP & CFO R. Richards said, “Taxable equivalent net interest income was $662 million,” and “net interest margin was 3.27%,” while describing the rate backdrop: “Earning asset yields fell faster than funding costs during the quarter… and loan repricing reflected the impact of the December rate cuts.”

Outlook

  • Richards framed rate sensitivity and near-term direction: “For the first quarter of 2027, our outlook for net interest income is moderately increasing given the uncertain path of benchmark rates,” and added, “The forward curve as of March 31 assumed no rate changes over the next 12 months.”
  • Richards provided a conditional growth estimate tied to that curve: “As that plays out, we estimate net interest income growth of about 7% to 8%, which would exceed our guide.”
  • On fees, Richards said, “For the first quarter of 2027, our outlook for adjusted customer-related fee income is moderately increasing versus the first quarter 2026 results of $174 million,” and added, “We currently expect results towards the top end of that range.”
  • On expenses, Richards said, “Our first quarter 2027 outlook for adjusted noninterest expense is moderately increasing versus the first quarter of 2026,” and reiterated the longer-year framework: “we continue to expect positive operating leverage for full year 2026 in the range of 100 to 150 basis points.”
  • Compared with the prior quarter’s guide language, management emphasized fewer expected rate cuts: Simmons said, “we were anticipating…rate cuts…in June and September. And based upon the forward curve, those are now off the table. So that having no cuts is embedded into our full year positive operating leverage guide.”

Financial Results

  • Simmons reported, “Net earnings were $232 million or $1.56 per diluted share,” and said the year-over-year change was “driven by revenue growth, a lower provision for credit losses and a lower effective tax rate.”
  • Simmons said quarter-over-quarter earnings declined “primarily reflecting lower revenue… and significantly lower securities gains as well as seasonal compensation expenses,” and noted, “There were no notable items in the first quarter with an impact greater than $0.05 per share.”
  • Richards reported balance-sheet profitability metrics: “Taxable equivalent net interest income was $662 million, down $21 million… from the prior quarter,” while “net interest margin was 3.27%, down 4 basis points linked quarter and up 17 basis points year-over-year.”
  • Richards said, “Adjusted noninterest expense was $558 million,” attributing the sequential increase to “seasonal compensation,” while describing year-over-year pressures including “increased marketing, technology costs, professional and outsourced services, and higher incentive compensation.”
  • On capital and distributions, Richards said, “The Common Equity Tier 1 ratio was 11.5%, flat during the quarter,” and added this reflected earnings partly offset by “the $77 million in common shares repurchased and dividends paid” and “growth in risk-weighted assets.”

Q&A

  • John Pancari, Evercore ISI: asked about loan-yield compression and “new money loan yields”; Executive VP & CFO Richards said the move was largely “benchmark repricing” after the “December rate cuts” and cited portfolio pricing: “we’re seeing a 72 basis point spread on the front book vis-a-vis the back book.”
  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: pressed on deposit-cost trajectory and competition; CEO Simmons said there is “still some trailing activities, some repricing down on term deposits,” while President & COO Scott McLean pointed to bringing “off-balance sheet deposits back on balance sheet…at rates that are attractive,” calling them “25 to 30, 35 basis points accretive to brokered deposits.”
  • Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: asked about buybacks and capital flexibility; CFO Richards said “the prospect of share repurchases are still on the table, subject to Board approval,” while noting the pending agency acquisition “would be a source of consuming capital.”
  • David Chiaverini, Jefferies: asked to quantify Basel III endgame benefits; CFO Richards said, “we would size that between 9% to 10% of RWA relief… about 93 basis points to common equity Tier 1.”
  • Anthony Elian, JPMorgan: asked whether M&A could broaden to bank deals; CEO Simmons said, “We’re waiting for fish to swim into the pond that we are comfortable with,” and added, “it’s not an objective to do M&A to grow.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone, centered on sensitivity to rates, deposit-cost trajectory, capital return capacity, and regulatory capital impacts, including questions asking management to “help us connect the dots” on deposits (Gosalia) and to “quantify” Basel III benefits (Chiaverini).
  • Neutral-to-slightly positive management tone in prepared remarks, paired with confident conditional language on NII and fees; Richards said management “currently expect results towards the top end of that range” on fee income, and Simmons said, “We are reasonably pleased with our performance.”
  • Versus the prior quarter, management’s tone reflected less reliance on imminent rate cuts; Simmons explicitly said expected cuts “are now off the table,” shifting emphasis toward balance-sheet actions, deposit initiatives, and pipelines.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance framing shifted from Q4’s rate-cut assumption to a no-change forward curve; in Q4, CFO Richards said 2026 guidance “assumes 225 basis point cuts…occurring in June and September,” while in Q1 management said the forward curve “assumed no rate changes” and Simmons said those cuts “are now off the table.”
  • Strategic narrative broadened from growth/efficiency and capital-return timing in Q4 to tangible M&A and product expansion in Q1, highlighted by the Basis agency-lending agreement and the “beyond the business” rollout.
  • Analyst focus remained consistent on deposits, margins, capital return, and loan growth quality, with incremental Q1 attention to Basel III endgame quantification and the agency/M&A strategy.

Risks and Concerns

  • On lending competition, management acknowledged pricing pressure: CFO Richards said, “probably pricing pressure on CRE,” and Chief Credit Officer Derek Steward said C&I pricing competition exists but “it’s not significant.”
  • On credit monitoring, Steward said attention is most focused on “the commercial and industrial space,” adding the bank is “not seeing a lot of impact from tariffs or from the events in the Middle East at this point, but watching.”
  • On execution and timing risk, management noted the Basis acquisition is “subject to regulatory and customary closing approvals.”

Final Takeaway

Management tied Q1 profitability and stable credit metrics to a strategy centered on capital markets fee growth, targeted deposit-gathering products (Gold Account and the new small-business “beyond the business” suite), and a pending agency-lending acquisition intended to deepen commercial real estate and capital markets capabilities. Guidance language shifted from rate-cut assumptions discussed last quarter to a no-rate-change forward curve, with management outlining conditional net interest income growth of about 7% to 8% over the next 12 months alongside “moderately increasing” fee income and expenses, while keeping share repurchases positioned as “still on the table” subject to approvals and acquisition-related capital needs.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Atos SE reports Q1 results; narrows FY26 organic revenue outlook
data.description:
• Atos SE press release (AEXAF): Q1 Revenue of €1.74B or €1.64B at go-forward perimeter. Organic revenue growth of -11% at the go-forward perimeter.
  • Liquidity at €1,736 million at the end of March.

  • FY 2026 outlook confirmed: Organic revenue growth range narrowed to -1% to -5%. Operating margin expected at c. 7%. Positive net change in cash.
